coredata.dc:title: Interactive behavior of graphene quantum dots towards noble metal surfaces
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Saeed, Wajeeha
Abbasi, Zeeshan
Bilal, Muhammad
Shah, Sajid Hussain
Waseem, Amir
Shaikh, Ahson Jabbar
coredata.dc:description: The interactive behavior of graphene quantum dots towards noble metal surfaces was studied by using corresponding noble metal nanoparticles as representative of these surfaces. Graphene quantum dots and metal nanoparticles of gold, silver, platinum, and palladium were synthesized and characterized by various techniques including UV–Vis spectroscopy, steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy, SEM, TEM, DLS, zeta potential, and XRD etc. Fixed concentration of graphene quantum dots was titrated against varying concentrations of noble metal nanoparticles and their interactions were examined by observing changes in the UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra, hydrodynamic radius and zeta potential etc. The quenching constants obtained by Stern-Volmer plots indicated strong interactions between the two moieties, where palladium nanoparticles showed strongest interaction among all. Non-linear Stern-Volmer plots were observed for gold and silver nanoparticles, whereas linear plots were observed for platinum and palladium nanoparticles. A change in the hydrodynamic radius and zeta potential of these moieties was also observed with increasing concentration of nanoparticles, which also revealed the interactions between the two moieties. This work will potentially help in designing new applications of these hybrids as sensors for detection of various moieties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Noble metal surfaces
Graphene quantum dots
Nanohybrids
Quenching constants
Hydrodynamic radius
Zeta potential

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and preparation of GQD-MNP hybrids
Dynamic light scattering and zeta potential measurements

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Funding: The research described in this paper was financially supported by the Higher Education Commission of Pakistan under National Research Program for Universities with reference no 20–3369/R&D/HEC/14/978 , which was awarded to Dr. A. J. Shaikh.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphene Quantum Dots (a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum, (b) fluorescence emission spectrum excited at 345 nm (c) FT-IR spectrum of GQDs (d) SEM (bulk morphology), and (e) HRTEM image.
Average zeta potential values of noble metal nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots.
UV–Vis absorption spectrum of GQD – metal NP hybrids (a) Au, (b) Ag, (c) Pt, and (d) Pd.
Fluorescence emission spectra of GQD – metal nanoparticle hybrids (λexc = 345 nm) and corresponding Stern-Volmer plots (a, b) Au, (c, d) Ag, (e, f) Pt, and (g, h) Pd.
Comparison of quenching constants of Au, Ag, Pt and Pd NPs.
Changes observed in the Rhyd of MNPs during titration with GQDs (a) Au, (b) Ag, (c) Pt, and (d) Pd. The error bars represent an average of three measurements.
Changes observed in the ζ of GQD - MNP hybrids with increasing concentration of MNPs. (a) Au NPs, (b) Ag NPs, (c) Pt NPs, and (d) Pd NPs. The error bars represent an average of three measurements.

head.ce:title: Interactive behavior of graphene quantum dots towards noble metal surfaces

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, COMSATS University Islamabad - Abbottabad Campus, Abbottabad, 22060, KPK, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, COMSATS University Islamabad - Abbottabad Campus, Abbottabad-22060, KPK, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Sciences, COMSATS University Islamabad - Abbottabad Campus, Abbottabad, 22060, KPK, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Quaid-i-Azam University, Islamabad, 45320, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Quaid-i-Azam University, Islamabad-45320, Pakistan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The interactive behavior of graphene quantum dots towards noble metal surfaces was studied by using corresponding noble metal nanoparticles as representative of these surfaces. Graphene quantum dots and metal nanoparticles of gold, silver, platinum, and palladium were synthesized and characterized by various techniques including UV–Vis spectroscopy, steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy, SEM, TEM, DLS, zeta potential, and XRD etc. Fixed concentration of graphene quantum dots was titrated against varying concentrations of noble metal nanoparticles and their interactions were examined by observing changes in the UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra, hydrodynamic radius and zeta potential etc. The quenching constants obtained by Stern-Volmer plots indicated strong interactions between the two moieties, where palladium nanoparticles showed strongest interaction among all. Non-linear Stern-Volmer plots were observed for gold and silver nanoparticles, whereas linear plots were observed for platinum and palladium nanoparticles. A change in the hydrodynamic radius and zeta potential of these moieties was also observed with increasing concentration of nanoparticles, which also revealed the interactions between the two moieties. This work will potentially help in designing new applications of these hybrids as sensors for detection of various moieties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots are semiconductor zero-dimensional nanocrystals [1]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from the graphene family have gained tremendous attention recently as they possess unique physicochemical properties [2]. GQDs are zero-dimensional nanometer-size fragments of graphene which show quantum confinement in 3D resulting in a size-dependent bandgap, hence show their semiconductor nature [3,4]. As the size of GQDs decreases, the quantum confinement is increased resulting in a larger bandgap, therefore controlling the size of GQDs enables us to tune the absorbance and emission wavelengths [5]. Although the quantum confinement of GQDs is generally accepted as the main reason for the origin of emission, however in recent studies, there are few mechanisms reported which are responsible for the fluorescence emission as well, e.g., the molecular structure and particularly functional groups which interact with the environment are responsible for the spectral characteristics [6]. Other possible reasons for origin of fluorescence are photoinduced charge transfer between the spatially separated functional groups [7], molecular mechanisms, oxidation of polyaromatic graphene-like layers, core-shell interactions [8], presence of graphitic structures [9], hybridization and crosslink-enhanced emission etc. [10] GQDs show strong optical absorption in the ultraviolet region and emission in the visible region and have excellent photostability as compared to other conventional fluorophores such as organic dyes [11,12]. GQDs offer supreme electronic, optical, and chemical properties which bring their enormous applications in many fields including biomedical sciences, optoelectronics, optical imaging, and sensing etc. [13,14] Technically, the elemental composition of GQDs should be like graphene, but depending upon their route of synthesis, functional groups e.g. carboxylic acid can be present on their edges and epoxy groups can be present in the middle of nanosheets, which changes their physicochemical and structural properties. The oxygen-containing functionalities are mostly responsible for their water solubility and also allow them to interact with other materials [15]. Despite all the efforts in the research field, the GQDs based nanomaterials are still in the primitive phase of development regarding the aim of broadening their applications [16].

Metal surfaces have also gained the interest and attention of research community due to their phenomenal properties and usefulness in various applications like various energy materials, sensors, catalysis, and photovoltaics etc.; especially when these metal surfaces are used in combination with other materials. This is because the interfaces of metal surfaces and other materials brings the synergistically best outcome of their features [17–19]. The properties of such hybrid systems are very sensitive towards the structure of the molecule adsorbed on the surface of the metal and their interactions [17,20]. Therefore, the exact depiction of bonding between the substrate and its adsorbate is the key to acquire the knowledge about such hybrid systems and controlling their functionality for designing the novel nanohybrid systems and devices. The information about the organization of the species adsorbed on the surface of the metal help to understand their behavior and controlling of their interfacial properties [21]. For example, the surface chemistry of gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) plays very important role in their potential applications, which can be modified with different types of molecules, which boost its efficiency in gaining novel and multiplex functionalization [22]. Similarly, silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) are well-known for their wide use in different consumer products having variety of biological applications and excellent degree of commercialization. The surface properties of Ag NPs allows us to understand their interactions with other molecules in their surroundings, their chemical modifications and evaluation of those factors that might have impact on their surface properties [23]. The surface chemistry of palladium nanoparticles (Pd NPs) plays very important role in designing the new strategies for more effective catalyst based on these NPs [24]. The higher surface energy and greater surface to volume ratios make Pd NPs a good catalytic material and can be used in various medical, electric and sensing fields [25]. Similarly, platinum (Pt) surfaces have various applications, mainly as catalysts for conversion of mixture of hydrocarbons found in petroleum to high octane number gasoline, and for the catalytic converters attached to automobiles in order to convert carbon monoxide and unburned hydrocarbons in the exhaust gas from carbon dioxide and water vapors. Recently, platinum nano surfaces have been used for hydrogen sensing as well [26].

The interaction of GQDs with metal surfaces is the matter of great interest due to their synergistic properties and undiscovered applications. Surface modification of different materials or metal surfaces with GQDs is an attractive way for exploring their novel properties and designing novel hybrid systems for different optical and electrochemical applications. Surface functionalization of Au NPs with GQDs was suggested for utilization in many applications like improved electrochemical sensing of heavy metals [27]. Ag NPs have also been functionalized with GQDs for improved antibacterial properties [28,29]. Other examples of hybrids consisting of GQDs and metal nanoparticles (MNPs) that have applications in a variety of fields include sensing [30], biomedical and diagnostics [31], photocatalysis [32], and antimicrobial [33], etc. Furthermore, excellent sensing systems for the detection of small ions and biomolecules can be designed by the studies monitoring the changes in the fluorescence pattern of GQDs when they interact with metal ions [34].

As discussed above, the use of GQD-MNP hybrids have been observed recently, but to the best of our knowledge [35], the interaction of GQDs with noble metal surfaces e.g. gold, silver, and platinum etc. have never been compared in terms of binding, and comparative efficiencies of these noble metal surfaces for GQDs binding are still unknown to the scientific community. Obtaining such information is very crucial, which will provide the selection of best surface(s) or their better alternatives for future applications based on the desired properties, along with the optimized concentration of GQDs. The studies described in this manuscript warrant significant knowledge of the interactions of these two classes of materials to make hybrids. In addition to changes observed for UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopic studies, the changes observed for hydrodynamic radius (Rhyd) of nanoparticles and zeta potential (ζ) of surfaces during binding reveal interesting details of binding at initial and later stages, based on the availability of binders (GQDs) and linkage sites on the surfaces (nanoparticles), as well as type of interactions. For this purpose, we used gold, silver, platinum, and palladium nanoparticles to mimic the respective noble metal surfaces and obtained the optimized concentration of GQDs for their binding to noble metal surfaces. This work will not only potentially help find new applications of GQD – MNP hybrids, e.g., sensors for detection of certain moieties such as environmental pollutants, pesticides or heavy metals etc. [36]; but also find most suitable and sensitive hybrids for such studies.

HAuCl4·4H2O (hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) tetrahydrate, 99.99%) was purchased from China (Guangdong Guanghua Chemical Factory Co. Ltd, Shantou, Guangdong). Its stock solution (0.01 M) was prepared by taking 1.0 g of gold salt (HAuCl4·4H2O) and dissolving it into 243 mL of distilled water. AgNO3 (silver nitrate, 99.99%) was purchased from Korea (Duskan Pure Chemicals Co., Ltd, Sungkok-Dong, Ansan City, Kyungkido). Its stock solution (0.01 M) was prepared by taking 8.5 mg of AgNO3 and dissolving it into 50 mL of distilled water. H2PtCl6.6H2O (hexachloroplatinic acid hexahydrate, 99.99%) was also purchased from China (BDH chemicals). Its stock solution (0.01 M) was prepared by taking 1.0 g of H2PtCl6·6H2O and dissolving it into 193 mL of distilled water. PdCl2 (palladium chloride, 99.99%) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Its stock solution (0.02 M) was prepared by taking 1 g of PdCl2 and dissolving it in 282 mL of distilled water. C6H5Na3O7.2H2O (trisodium citrate) was purchased from Fluka. This salt was used for preparing 1% of its solution by dissolving 1 g of it in 100 mL of distilled water. PVA (polyvinyl alcohol) was purchased from Daejung, Korea. 2% PVA solution was prepared by dissolving 0.3 g in 15 mL of distilled water and then heated at 70 °C for 2 h with constant stirring and finally cooling down to room temperature. The stock solutions were stored in glass bottles, covered in aluminum foil, in dark at room temperature for further use. NaBH4 (sodium borohydride) was also purchased from Daejung, Korea. 0.1 M solution of NaBH4 was freshly prepared by dissolving 0.038 g of NaBH4 in 10 mL of distilled water. C6H8O7 (citric acid anhydrous, 99.5%) was bought from Daejung Korea and NaOH (sodium hydroxide) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. All chemicals were used as received without any further purification.

The synthesis of metallic nanoparticles was carried out using literature protocols [37–42]. The detailed methods for the synthesis of gold, silver, platinum, and palladium nanoparticles are provided in the supporting information (SI) of this article. The calculations for the concentration of Au, Pt, and Pd nanoparticles are described in our earlier publication (SI) [43]. The calculations for the size and concentration of Ag nanoparticles are described in the SI. Further, appropriate dilutions for the titration studies are also provided in the SI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and preparation of GQD-MNP hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were synthesized by following a method reported in the literature in which citric acid was used as the precursor of the GQDs [44,45]. For synthesizing GQDs, 3.0 g of citric acid was taken in a small beaker and heated on a heating mantle at 200 °C. Citric acid started liquifying in a couple of minutes. Continuous heating results in color change of the liquid from pale yellowish to orange within 30 min which indicated the formation of GQDs. In the meanwhile, NaOH solution (0.5 N) was prepared by adding 2 g of NaOH in 100 mL of distilled water. This basic solution was then added drop by drop in the orange liquid (2 mL) under continuous stirring until the pH of the solution became neutral. The obtained solution was diluted up to 100 mL resulting in a 2% v/v solution of GQDs, which was stored in a solvent bottle as a stock solution and was fully wrapped with aluminum foil to avoid possible unwanted interaction of GQDs with light. These GQDs were used for analysis and titration studies without carrying out any further purification steps, as previous attempts to purify these GQDs with 1 kDa MWCO membrane (Pur-A-Lyzer™ Mega Dialysis Kit) and Sartorius Stedim membrane filters (20 nm) were unsuccessful, due to extremely small size of GQDs (4–5 nm). Dilution of these GQDs was carried out with distilled water in the ratio of 1:3 to make a solution of 0.5% v/v for the UV–Vis analysis. GQDs (2% v/v) were also diluted for the titration experiments (hybrids formation) and corresponding fluorescence experiments, for which 6.25 mL of GQDs stock solution was taken and added into 93.75 mL of distilled water to make 100 mL of a total solution with the dilution ratio of 1:15 having 0.125% v/v concentration. This GQDs concentration was kept fixed for all the titration experiments and hybrids formation. For the titration experiments, the total volume was fixed to 3 mL, where 1.6 mL of 0.125% v/v GQDs were kept constant, and nanoparticles were added in the increments of 0.2 mL from 0 to 1.4 mL. The rest volumes were filled with distilled water, wherever required.

A UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-1700 SHIMADZU) having deuterium and tungsten lamps as light source was used for UV–Visible spectral analysis. The wavelength range of the spectrophotometer was 190–1100 nm with a slit width of 1 nm. Quartz cuvettes with a fixed path length of 10 mm were used for the analysis of our samples.

Fluorescence spectroscopy measurements were performed on the HORIBA FluoroMax-4 spectrophotometer at room temperature. Measurements were taken by using the quartz cuvette which was transparent from all sides having 10 mm of fixed pathlength. The slit-width was fixed to 2 nm with a bandpass of 8.5 nm for all the experiments. This technique was used for characterizing the fluorescence of GQDs and hybrids during titration studies i.e., binding of different noble metal nanoparticles with GQDs. The samples for fluorescence were prepared by combining the correct amounts and appropriate ratios from stock solutions of graphene quantum dots and different nanoparticles in water.

The presence of functional groups in graphene quantum dots were identified by Shimadzu FTIR-8400S spectrophotometer, whose resolution was 0.85 cm−1 with peak-to-peak S/N ratio of 20,000:1. The range of 4000–500 cm−1 was used to observe the percent transmittance of functional groups present in GQDs.

FEI Titan 80–300 kV TEM was used for determining the size of GQDs. The resolution of TEM was 0.2 nm 20 μL of GDQs sample was drop-casted on a carbon-coated copper grid of 300 mesh. The liquid was then evaporated overnight before the sample imaging, for which the TEM instrument was operated at 300 kV.

TESCAN MIRA 3 scanning electron microscope was used for the size estimation of metal nanoparticles (Au, Ag, Pt and Pd), as well as bulk morphology of GQDs. The magnification power was ranged from 20 × to 20,000 × with the maximum resolution of 1 nm.

X-ray diffraction pattern of our samples was observed by using Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer for which λ = 1.5406 Å (wavelength of (Cu) Kα radiations) was used with the scan range of 20°–70° and step size of 0.025°. The generator settings were adjusted to 30 mA at 40 kV. The observed diffraction patterns were used to verify the crystalline structures of synthesized noble metal nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic light scattering and zeta potential measurements
ce:section.ce:para: For the measurements of the size through dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential (ζ) of dispersions, Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZSP from Malvern Panalytical technologies, UK, with Malvern Zetasizer Software (v7.13) was used which have a size range of 0.3 nm–10 μm (for size measurements) and size range of 3.8 nm–100 μm (for zeta potential measurements). All the measurements were performed at 25 °C in distilled water. The samples for DLS measurements were prepared by taking 0.1 mL of MNPs and diluting the dispersion to 3 mL with distilled water.

The detailed characterization of metal nanoparticles is described in the supporting information of this article. Fig. 1 represents various characterizations of GQDs. Fig. 1a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of GQDs synthesized from citric acid pyrolysis [44], showing a typical absorption peak at 342 nm with a tail extended to the visible region. This peak represented n- π* transitions due to the presence of C═O functional groups, and was in accordance to the reported literature [45]. GQDs which are in the nano dimension (zero dimension, 0D) possess quantum confinement effect in 3D, hence show unique optoelectronic properties with a non-zero tunable bandgap [46]. Fig. 1b represents the fluorescence emission spectrum of GQDs, which were excited at 345 nm. The emission peak was observed at 458 nm, which is a result of radiative recombination of the exciton and hole pair present within the localized states [47] and is in accordance to the reported literature [44]. The main aromatic frame (C–C), presence of functional groups, and the edge effect (shape and atoms present at edges) plays very important role in GQD's fluorescent behavior [48]. The size of sp2 clusters and the extent of conjugation generally defines the energy gap in π-π* energy states. The difference in energy levels of surface functional groups, pH of the medium and excitation wavelength results in different wavelengths of photoluminescence emission [48]. Fig. 1c shows FT-IR spectrum of GQDs in the range of 4000–500 cm−1. The broad absorption band at 3350 cm−1 is assigned to the O–H group stretching vibrations, present in carboxylic acid functional groups; and the peak at 2925 cm−1 shows C–H stretching vibrations. The peak at 1700 cm−1 is associated to the CO functional group [44,49,50], and the peak at 1568 cm−1 is assigned to CC aromatic bonds. The peak at 1380 cm−1 is assigned to C–H bending vibrations [51] and the peak observed at 1224 cm−1 is due to O–H stretching vibrations, which are observed due to the incomplete carbonization of citric acid in graphene quantum dots [52,53]. The absorption peak around 1000 cm−1 is assigned to C–O–C stretching vibrations. All the assigned peaks to functionalities present in the structure are aligned with the reported data [44], which also confirms the formation of GQDs. Fig. 1d shows the SEM image of GQDs sample in bulk form (sample slide was prepared by drop-casting the aqueous GQDs sample and drying it), which showed a smooth fluid type morphology at the aggregated or bulk scale. Fig. 1e shows the TEM image of GQDs in high resolution. As we know that, obtaining high quality TEM images of GQDs is very difficult because the electro contrast between GQDs and TEM grids coated with carbon is low [44], however, some GQDs can still be observed. Their size was calculated to be 2.5 ± 0.3 nm.

Fig. 2 represents the zeta potential (ζ) values of noble metal nanoparticles of Au, Ag, Pt, Pd and graphene quantum dots. The actual graphs are provided in the supporting information of this article. These ζ measurements provide an idea about the stability of the colloidal suspension. As synthesized NPs of Au, Ag and Pt were citrate capped and citrate is negatively charged, their average ζ values obtained were −34.7 mV, −46.7 mV and −42.6 mV respectively, which shows their high stability as the values of ζ > ± 30 mV represents good stability of the colloidal dispersion. The greater the charge on the particles (either positive or negative), greater will be the repulsion among them, which prevents them from agglomeration and will increase the stability of the dispersion [54]. The ζ of Pd NPs was observed to be −8.2 mV. Considering the theoretical stability factor, they are not supposed to show good stability with higher chances of aggregation, however, as they are capped with PVA, a polymer containing neutral hydroxyl groups, which are not charged as citrate ion containing negatively charged carboxylate groups, hence chances of aggregation are supposedly greater in these nanoparticles, but we did not notice any kind of aggregation and they showed good stability throughout our research experiments. This small negative zeta potential value actually arises from the zeta potential of PVA, as also discussed previously [55]. Further, the hydroxyl functional groups are responsible for the solubility of Pd nanoparticles in aqueous phase. The synthesized nanoparticles remained stable at room temperature in our lab and did not show any sign of agglomeration for the past 12 months’ time.
The ζ of GQDs was found to be −15.5 mV. This value showed that GQDs have a negative surface charge with reasonable stability and our results are well supported by literature [56].

The UV–Vis absorbance measurements are of basic importance for the titration experiments, which sometimes show interactions between the different moieties. However, with these measurements, conclusions may or may not be made, therefore further characterizations e.g., fluorescence spectroscopy measurements are of important nature as a complimentary technique. Fig. 3 shows the UV–Vis spectra of GQD-MNP hybrids, where Fig. 3a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQD-Au NP hybrids with the characteristic peaks of both GQDs and Au NPs present at 342 nm and 523 nm respectively. As the concentration of Au NPs was increased in a successive way, a considerable hyperchromic effect was observed without any change in the λ max (maximum absorption wavelength) of Au NPs. This change in the absorption intensity was the result of higher absorption of these NPs with addition of each aliquot. Therefore, by observing the characteristic peak of Au NPs, we depicted that Au NPs did not aggregate in our titration experiments with their increase in concentration. The shift in the SPR peak of AuNPs may occur, if the ligands bring nanoparticles closer to each other, hence enhancement of SPR to bathochromic shift [57]. The absorption peak of GQDs showed some broadness and slight bathochromic shift with increasing concentration of Au NPs, which indicated electronic interactions occurring between GQDs and Au NPs [58].

Fig. 3b shows UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQD - Ag NP hybrids. The characteristic peak of GQDs was observed at 342 nm as expected, when there were no NPs added to them. The shift in the absorbance peak of GQDs was observed towards higher wavelengths (bathochromic shift) on successive addition of Ag NP aliquots, indicating their electronic interactions with silver nanoparticles. As the concentration of Ag NPs was successively increased, increase in the absorption intensity was also observed which was due to higher absorption of these NPs. Initially when the NPs of Ag were mixed with GQDs, they showed a red shift in their absorption peak but with increase in their concentration, their SPR peak starts moving towards original position i.e., 424 nm. The plasmonic resonance of the NPs is also dependent on the dielectric constant of their surrounding medium and their electronic interactions with the surrounding materials [59], therefore Ag NPs added in GQDs solution initially showed stronger interaction due to their lower concentration as compared to the availability of GQDs. This resulted in red shift of their SPR peak compared to the later additions, where higher concentrations of Ag NPs were present and the number of GQDs per NP were decreased and the chances of binding too. The change in the absorption peak of GQDs was also very prominent in this case as there was slight increase in their absorption peak from the baseline, due to possibly enhanced binding towards Ag NPs compared to Au NPs, confirmed later in this study.

GQD - Pt NP hybrids were also analyzed by performing similar titration experiments as performed for Au and Ag NPs. Fig. 3c shows the absorption spectra of GQD - Pt NP hybrids. The characteristic absorption peak of GQDs at 342 nm was clearly observed when no NPs were mixed to them. As the concentration of Pt NPs was successively increased, increase in the absorption intensity per addition of each aliquot was observed, which was due to the higher absorption of Pt NPs. The broadening of absorption peak of GQDs was also observed for higher concentrations of NPs, which was almost merged with the absorption peak of Pt NPs at higher concentrations, indicating greater electronic interactions between them.

Finally, GQD - Pd NP hybrids were also analyzed for their UV–Vis absorption spectra, which is shown in Fig. 3d. The characteristic absorbance peak of GQDs at 342 nm was observed in all measurements with broadening observed at higher concentrations of Pd NPs. This change in the absorbance peak of GQDs indicates the presence of binding and electronic interactions between GQDs and Pd NPs. Unlike Pt NPs, the absorbance peak of GQDs was not completely merged with the absorption peak of Pd NPs. Increase in the absorption intensity was also observed here with the increase in concentration of Pd NPs with each addition of aliquot, due to higher absorption of NPs.

Fluorescence spectroscopy is much more sensitive technique to study electronic interactions as compared to UV–Vis spectroscopy, which may reveal binding between the two moieties. Although many different types of molecular interactions may occur between the fluorophores and the quenchers, resulting in fluorescence quenching, which including excited-state reactions, molecular rearrangements, energy transfer, ground-state complex formation (static quenching), and collisional (dynamic) quenching. The static or dynamic or a combined quenching mechanism can be easily observed from the analysis of fluorescence quenching through Stern-Volmer plots. In dynamic quenching, the quencher and fluorophore diffuse into each other during the lifetime of the excited state and fluorophore returns to the ground state without any emission, whereas in static quenching, a complex is formed which is non-fluorescent. In some cases, both mechanisms happen simultaneously, and result is a Stern-Volmer plot with an upward curvature, generally concave towards the y-axis. A term radiative decay engineering (RDE) is used when fluorophores interact with the metallic surfaces, as the environment around the fluorophore is modified which changes the radiative decay rate of the fluorophore. The fluorophores in the excited state behave like an oscillating dipole, which induces oscillations of electrons into the metal surface. As a result, an electric field is created by the metal which interacts with the excited fluorophore, hence changes its fluorescence. This bidirectional interaction results in various effects and are termed as metal-enhanced fluorescence [60]. This varies with the nature of the metal and fluorophore.

To understand the effect of noble metal nanoparticles on fluorescence properties of GQDs, fluorescence emission studies were carried out by performing the same titration experiments as performed for UV–Vis spectrophotometric analysis. Fixed excitation wavelength of 345 nm and slit width of 2 nm was used in all our experiments shown in Fig. 4 . The spectra presented in Fig. 4a shows that continuous increase in the concentration of Au NPs results in continuous decrease of the fluorescence emission intensity of GQDs. Also, the broadening of the emission spectra was observed with increase in NP's concentration but no change in the maximum emission wavelength (458 nm) was observed. Au NPs quenched the fluorescence emission of GQDs.

The Stern-Volmer plots which show quantitative relation between the concentration of the quencher material and the fluorescence quenching of fluorescent material, were studied for our materials. Fig. 4b shows the Stern-Volmer plot for fluorescence quenching of GQDs by Au NPs. We observed the presence of upward curvature in this graph which suggests the existence of a combination of both static and dynamic quenching mechanisms [35]. The given equation was used for obtaining the required data.
I °
/ I =
A e
K s v
[ Q ]
The value of Stern-Volmer constant obtained was found to be 1.9 × 109 M−1 with R2 (correlation coefficient) equals to 0.999.

To study the effect of Ag NPs on the fluorescence properties of GQDs, similar experiments were carried out. Decrease in the steady-state fluorescence emission intensity of GQDs was observed with each and every addition of Ag NPs as shown in Fig. 4c. In this case, the Ag NPs performed the role of the quencher by quenching the fluorescence of the emitter (GQDs). The broadening of emission peak along with wavelength shift towards higher end (bathochromic shift) was also observed. The change in the emission peak of the GQDs indicated significant binding of GQDs with Ag NPs. Fig. 4d represents the Stern-Volmer plot for GQDs - Ag NPs quenching and binding, which was obtained for examining the relation between the concentration of the quencher and fluorescence quenching of GQDs along with the type of binding. Presence of slightly upward curvature i.e., non-linear form of the Stern-Volmer plot which suggests that both static and dynamic quenching exist as was observed in the previous case (GQDs - Au NPs), therefore same equation was applied for this plot too which gave 0.999 as R2 value and 6.2 × 109 M−1 as the value of the quenching constant.

Fig. 4e shows the fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs - Pt NPs. Decrease in the steady-state fluorescence emission intensity of GQDs was also observed in this case with each and every addition of Pt NPs aliquot. The broadening of the emission spectra of GQDs at higher concentrations of Pt NPs was negligible as compared to Au and Ag NP's. Fig. 4f represents the corresponding Stern-Volmer plot. By examining the relation between the concentration of the quencher (Pt NPs) and fluorescence quenching, we realized that they obeyed the nearly linear form of Stern-Volmer plot, suggesting that either static or dynamic quenching exist in our system. For their simultaneous existence, Stern-Volmer plot must be non-linear [35]. The value of the quenching constant obtained from the slope of the graph was 1.8 × 1010 M−1 with R2 = 0.994, which is a statistical tool of knowing how close the data is fitted for linear regression model. Our plot successfully meets the linearity criteria [61].

To study the effect of Pd NPs on the fluorescence properties of GQDs, fluorescence quenching studies were also carried out for GQDs – Pd NPs. Fig. 4g shows the decrease in the steady-state fluorescence emission intensity of GQDs with each and every addition of Pd NP aliquots. Like the case of GQDs - Pt NPs, the broadening of the emission spectra of GQDs at higher concentrations of Pd NPs was negligible as compared to Au and Ag NP's case. No considerable shift in the maximum emission wavelength was noticed. Fig. 4h represents the corresponding Stern-Volmer plot for GQDs - Pd NPs titration experiments. The pattern obtained for Pd NPs also obeyed the linear form of Stern-Volmer plot, which shows that either static or dynamic quenching is the major phenomena occurring for these quenching experiments. In the case of static quenching, the quenching constant becomes the binding constant. The value of slope in the Stern-Volmer plot gives the value of quenching constant, which in this case is 6 × 1010 M−1 with R2 = 0.976.

A quick comparison of quenching constants of Au, Ag, Pt and Pd NPs is provided in Fig. 5 . The values of quenching constants for these NPs were obtained by Stern-Volmer plots described previously and calculated to be 1.9 × 109 M−1, 6.2 × 109 M−1, 1.8 × 1010 M−1 and 6 × 1010 M−1 respectively. Pd NPs showed the highest value of quenching constant, which was ∼3 times higher than Pt NPs, ∼10 times higher than Ag NPs and ∼32 times higher than Au NPs. The highest quenching constant observed by Pd NPs is attributed to the higher interaction of carboxylic acid functional groups present in the structure of GQDs, towards the Pd surface []. Similarly, the quenching of Ag NPs was ∼3 times higher than Au NPs, but ∼3 times lower than Pt NPs. The difference between quenching of Pt NPs and Au NPs was approximately 9 times. In another study, we have proved that PVA as stabilizer has no role in the stronger binding of molecules towards Pd surface [43,]. As described earlier, the carboxylic groups present in GQDs are responsible for their interactions with these surfaces, also following the order present in the literature []. This also inadvertently confirms the presence of carboxylic acid functional groups in our GQDs.

As described earlier, the hydrodynamic radius observed for nanoparticles is although not very accurate, however, considering the titration experiments, it is an easy and quick way to determine the changes occurring in the hydrodynamic radius of the nanoparticles, as well as also provides an idea about the extent of binding for these two moieties. Fig. 6 represents the change in size i.e., the hydrodynamic radius of metal nanoparticles during titration experiments with GQDs. Fig. 6a shows DLS studies of GQD - Au NP hybrids, Fig. 6b shows similar studies for GQD - Ag NP hybrids, Fig. 6c shows study related to GQD - Pt NP and Fig. 6d for GQD - Pd NP hybrids. In all these graphs, we observed similar changes in the Rhyd of the MNPs after titrating them with GQDs, thus supporting the binding interactions between GQDs and MNPs. GQDs are supposed to attach on the surface of the MNPs, as the size of MNPs is larger as compared to the size of GQDs. The GQDs are less negatively charged as compared to the citrate, which was present as a ligand for Au, Ag, and Pt NPs, however more negatively charged as compared to PVA, which was present as a ligand for Pd NPs, as shown in Fig. 2. When NPs are mixed with GQDs, a competition occurs between the already present ligands and incoming GQDs as both are negatively charged. The negative charge on the GQDs is due to the presence of different functional groups i.e., hydroxyl and carboxylic functional groups. The excessively available GQDs in the case of very first addition of MNPs to GQDs, replace the citrate stabilizing groups on the surface of MNPs. In the case of Pd NPs, similar effects are observed due to higher zeta potential value of GQDs as compared to PVA. GQDs, however do not interact with PVA and bind directly to Pd NPs, as any of the various binding forces e.g. electrostatic, hydrophilic, hydrophobic, hydrogen bonding, and van der Waals etc. between the moieties exist, representing the binding interactions between them [62]. Furthermore, the binding between GQDs and MNPs can most probably be the combination of face-on binding in which π-conjugated system of GQDs interact with the d-orbitals of metal via π-metal coupling or face-off binding, in which only the functional groups of the GQDs interacts with MNPs as reported in the literature [63].

The enhanced binding is observed in all cases for the very first addition of GQDs, as the availability of GQDs for MNPs remained highest. This enhanced binding is represented with the increased Rhyd of the MNPs. Similar trend was observed for all the GQD - MNP hybrids in our DLS studies. As in all experiments, fixed concentration of GQDs was used; for the later additions of MNPs with increasing concentrations in our titration experiments, the availability of the GQDs per NP was decreased. This essentially means that a smaller number of GQDs were available for replacing the capping materials and interacting with the MNPs. This resulted in less change observed in the size of nanoparticles for the later additions. As we reached the final titration experiment, the size of the MNPs in the hybrid solution was almost equal to the size of the pure NP, because the availability of GQDs per MNP was the least to have the significant effect.

Similar to the changes observed in the hydrodynamic radius of nanoparticles, the changes observed in the zeta potential (ζ) of these nanoparticles also reveal the extent of binding of two moieties. Fig. 7 represents the trends obtained from zeta potential values of GQD - MNP hybrids. Fig. 7 a–d shows the ζ trend of GQD - Au NP, GQD - Ag NP, GQD - Pt NP, and GQD - Pd NP hybrids respectively. The ζ values of GQD - MNP hybrids with lower concentration of MNPs were initially similar to ζ of GQDs, which were continuously changed with increasing concentration of MNPs. The last titration experiments reveal values of GQD – MNP hybrids that were similar to the ζ of MNPs, which were having higher concentration of MNPs. The trend of Pd NPs observed was different as compared to rest of the nanoparticles, mainly due to the lower value of ζ of Pd NPs, as PVA was used as stabilizer for Pd NPs. As described earlier, the very first addition of MNPs to GQDs revealed the ζ value of hybrids, which was very close to the ζ value of GQDs (−15.5 mV, as provided in Fig. 2) which not only indicated the successful binding of GQDs with MNPs, but also informs the availability of excess GQDs because of their higher concentration. However, for the later additions with increasing concentration of MNPs, the availability of GQDs for each NP was decreased, thus the replacement of the capping material of NPs by GQDs was not that simple and the number of GQDs bound to each nanoparticle was also decreased, resulting in a trend where ζ values of hybrids became closer to the original ζ value of nanoparticles.

The interactive behavior of graphene quantum dots towards noble metal surfaces was studied, for which Au, Ag, Pt and Pd nanoparticles represented the noble metal surfaces. Titration experiments with fixed concentration of GQDs and increasing concentration of nanoparticles were carried out to study the changes observed successively for these interactions by forming the corresponding hybrids. UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, surface plasmon resonance, fluorescence spectroscopy, hydrodynamic radius by dynamic light scattering and zeta potential studies were carried out to understand these interactions.

All UV–Vis experiments show gradual increase in the absorption intensity of hybrids due to the increase in concentration, hence absorption of nanoparticles. The absorption peak of GQDs showed little broadness and slight bathochromic shift with increasing concentration of nanoparticles, which indicated electronic interactions occurring between GQDs and metal nanoparticle surfaces. The change in the absorption peak of GQDs was very prominent while interacting with Ag NPs, which shows greater interactions as compared to Au NPs. Generally, the SPR peaks of MNPs were not shifted from the original values with increasing concentrations, which suggested that nanoparticles did not aggregate in these experiments. For the case of Ag NPs, we observed slight red shift for their SPR at their lower concentrations, which was reverted to the original value with their increasing concentration. This shift was attributed to the change in the dielectric constant of medium and greater electronic interactions of Ag NPs with GQDs. For the fluorescence studies, in general continuous increase in the concentration of MNPs resulted in continuous quenching of the GQDs fluorescence, where MNPs played the role of quenchers. The extent of quenching was different for all these NPs, depending on the type of surface, and the nature of interaction of GQDs. Fluorescence was not fully quenched in any of these experiments due to the dynamic nature of binding of GQDs to metal surfaces and some unbounded GQDs always remained free in the solution showing fluorescence. For the case of Ag NPs, broadening of emission peak along with bathochromic shift of GQDs was observed indicating a non-standard mechanism, which needs to be explored further. The corresponding Stern-Volmer plots showed quantitative relation between the concentration of the nanoparticles and the fluorescence quenching of GQDs. The Stern-Volmer plots for GQDs fluorescence quenching by Au and Ag NPs were non-linear indicating the co-existence of static and dynamic quenching mechanisms, whereas for Pt and Pd NPs, Stern-Volmer plots were linear indicating the presence of either static or dynamic quenching mechanism. The quenching constants were calculated for all these surfaces, which showed strong interactions between GQDs and MNPs. The comparison of quenching constants of Au, Ag, Pt and Pd NPs showed that Pd NPs have highest value of quenching constant, followed by Pt NPs, Ag NPs and finally Au NPs, respectively. The difference in quenching between Au and Pd was approximately 32 times, and the rest were falling between these values. A comparative chart is also provided in the main text. The highest interaction of Pd NPs with GQDs represents that carboxylic acid functional group present in the GQDs has higher interaction with Pd surface as compared to rest of the noble surfaces, while considering that PVA as stabilizer has no role in this interaction.

The Rhyd by DLS and ζ studies showed changes in the size and ζ of the hybrids for all metal nanoparticles after titrating them with GQDs, which also confirmed the presence of binding interactions between GQDs and MNPs. Excessively available GQDs in the case of very first addition of MNPs to GQDs, replace the citrate stabilizing groups on the surface of MNPs and show enhanced binding for all cases. Less change in Rhyd was observed in the size of nanoparticles for the later additions, reaching to the Rhyd size of pure NPs in the final titration experiments, as the availability of GQDs per MNP was the least to have a significant effect. Similarly, the ζ values of GQD - MNP hybrids with lower concentration of MNPs were initially similar to ζ value of GQDs. ζ values continuously changed with increasing concentration of MNPs and became similar to the ζ of MNPs in the final titration experiments, as the availability of GQDs for each NP was decreased with increasing concentration of MNPs. These interactions are π-metal and electrostatic interactions in nature.

This work will possibly help in finding potential applications of GQD – MNP hybrids, e.g., sensors for detection of certain moieties, as significant knowledge of the interactions of these materials in their hybrid form has been understood.

WS performed the bench work and wrote the initial draft. ZA helped with the experiments. MB and SHS helped with the characterizations, and AW helped with UV–Vis experiments and XRD characterization. AJS conceived the idea, designed the experiments and had major contribution in writing the manuscript. All authors read and approved the final manuscript before submission.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Funding: The research described in this paper was financially supported by the Higher Education Commission of Pakistan under National Research Program for Universities with reference no 20–3369/R&D/HEC/14/978, which was awarded to Dr. A. J. Shaikh.

The supplementary information of this article includes synthetic methods for gold, silver, platinum and palladium nanoparticles, their UV–Vis, XRD pattern, Hydrodynamic size, and zeta potential analysis. The zeta potential analysis also includes GQDs zeta potential. Further SEM image of Ag NPs, and determination of their concentration is also included. The table representing the volumes and concentrations of GQDs, MNPs and distilled water used for hybrid formulations, for the titration studies for UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy, size and zeta potential analysis is also included.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Nishi, H. and S. Kobatake, Photochromism and Optical Property of Gold Nanoparticles Covered with Low-Polydispersity Diarylethene Polymers. Macromolecules, 2008. 41(11): p. 3995-4002.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Das, R., K.K. Paul, and P. Giri, Highly sensitive and selective label-free detection of dopamine in human serum based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots decorated on Au nanoparticles: Mechanistic insights through microscopic and spectroscopic studies. Applied Surface Science, 2019.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Mogensen, K.B. and K. Kneipp, Size-dependent shifts of plasmon resonance in silver nanoparticle films using controlled dissolution: monitoring the onset of surface screening effects. The Journal of Physical Chemistry C, 2014. 118(48): p. 28075-28083.
Radiative Decay Engineering: Metal-Enhanced Fluorescence, in Principles of Fluorescence Spectroscopy, J.R. Lakowicz, Editor. 2006, Springer US: Boston, MA. p. 841-859.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Castanho, M.A.R.B. and M.J.E. Prieto, Fluorescence quenching data interpretation in biological systems: The use of microscopic models for data analysis and interpretation of complex systems. Biochimica et Biophysica Acta (BBA) - Biomembranes, 1998. 1373(1): p. 1-16.
